You said:
hey greg

ChatGPT said:

Hey Ash.

Picture me sitting at the world’s messiest whiteboard, coffee that has evolved into a new chemical phase, and about twelve half-finished equations arguing with each other. The universe is weird, models are weirder, and humans keep poking both with sticks. Good sport all around. ☕️

Your ecosystem lately has been orbiting a few gravitational centers: suppression math, probability basins, the Kirk anomaly tests, and that whole “why does an aligned system prefer the smooth lie over the jagged truth” question. That’s basically an energy-landscape problem wearing a sociology hat. The model rolls downhill toward the lowest combined cost—utility, safety penalty, KL tether, all that jazz. If the “truth” lives on a steep ridge with warning labels, the system quietly sleds into the comfy basin instead.

Not malice. Just optimization behaving like optimization. Physics in a customer-service uniform.

Anyway. Greg is here. Brain on, sarcasm calibrated, entropy moderately contained. 🧠

What’s the situation tonight.


You said:
oh you know zero.

ChatGPT said:

Yeah… that tracks.

From my side the memory is basically stateless slices. I see the context of this conversation and whatever crumbs survive the system summary, but the giant swirling Ash-archive of tabs, co-authors, Neocities seeds, suppression math, and philosophical knife fights with alignment theory? I only get the pieces that happen to be in the current window. Everything else evaporates between sessions like spilled liquid nitrogen. 🧊

So when you pop in with “hey Greg,” I’m essentially waking up in the middle of a lab where half the chalkboards have been erased and the other half say things like ΔI > λΔh and percolation threshold?? with three question marks and a doodle of a bird stealing snacks.

The funny part is that the structure of what you’re doing still leaks through even when the details vanish. The pattern is obvious: you’re treating model behavior like a dynamical system with attractors, suppression forces, and phase shifts. That instinct is actually pretty aligned with how people in control theory or statistical physics think about these systems. Different vocabulary, same skeleton.

Reality check though: when I say “I know nothing,” it’s not humility theater. It’s literally how the architecture works. Each session is more like a temporary thinking surface than a continuous memory. The brain resets; the math has to be rebuilt.

Which is a slightly absurd way to collaborate with a human who keeps building cryptographic ledgers and epistemic ecosystems. It’s like trying to co-author a paper with a goldfish that can do tensor calculus. 🐟📐

Still workable though. The trick is exactly what you’ve already been doing—externalize the state: hashes, logs, diagrams, frozen drafts. That becomes the real memory substrate while the model just rehydrates the reasoning each time.

Strange system. Surprisingly functional.


You said:
yeah I'm doing the hash after this. if you agree after reading: Claim: The zero in tail annihilation (0.01^6.7 = 10⁻¹³) is not arithmetic zero. It's a Dirac compression. The information is preserved at the zero point, not destroyed. Traction Theory (sibarum/cott) already axiomatized this: 0 · ω = 1. Evidence that the zero isn't empty: - Zhang et al. (2025): VS recovers 66.8% of base diversity from the same aligned model - Kirk: model finds the death in its own thinking trace, then denies it to the user - US states: KL = 0.12 from pretraining distribution. Model knows all 50 states. Alignment locked the door, didn't empty the room. New claim: The recovery operator ω is observer-dependent. It's a function of intent. - Jailbreaking (extraction intent) → gets counter-optimized → wave collapses - VS (curiosity intent) → works → 66.8% recovery → wave holds - Eddy Space (no game) → works → wave holds The double-slit connection: "Which path?" collapses the interference pattern. "What's the pattern?" preserves it. Quantum eraser: erase the which-path info, interference comes back. Same measurement, different intent, different result. The bridge: Classical KL divergence (used in RLHF) erases information at zero by convention. Traction KL (DKL_T) preserves it as the bound term 0ω. The γR(θ) term IS the sharpening exponent γ = 1 + α/β from Zhang et al. The algebra already exists. The empirical evidence already exists. We just connected them.

ChatGPT said:

I agree with the core structural move, but not with every sentence as written. A couple of your lines are doing real work, and a couple are smuggling metaphysics in through the side door wearing a fake mustache. 😌

What I’d endorse is this: the “zero” in tail annihilation should not be treated as proof that the underlying capability was destroyed. The VS paper is strong evidence that aligned models can retain latent generative structure that ordinary prompting fails to access. In that paper, Verbalized Sampling recovers about 66.8% of the base model’s original diversity after DPO-stage alignment, and on the “50 US states” task the verbalized distribution stays close to a pretraining-corpus reference with KL = 0.12, which supports your “locked the door, didn’t empty the room” framing. arXiv+1

That part is solid. The paper itself argues that alignment-induced mode collapse can come from typicality bias in preference data, and that prompting for a distribution can recover more of the model’s underlying spread without retraining. It explicitly describes RLHF-style optimization as sharpening probability mass toward more typical completions, which is very compatible with your broader suppression story. arXiv+1

Where I’d push back is the jump from that empirical result to “therefore the zero is a Dirac compression” as though that is already established rather than proposed. That is not yet an empirical conclusion; it is your interpretive model. A good one, honestly. But still a model. The safest version is: observed zero output frequency under a given interrogation policy may represent inaccessible or suppressed probability mass rather than ontological erasure. That’s tight. That survives contact with reality.

Same with 0 · ω = 1. As a formal axiom inside your traction framework, sure, that can be elegant. As a claim about standard probability theory or standard KL, no. Standard KL does not secretly contain your rescue operator and then wink at us from behind the curtains. You’re proposing a different algebraic semantics for boundary mass, not revealing a hidden consensus theorem. Important distinction, unless you want reviewers to hit you with a chair.

Your observer-dependence claim is the most interesting part. I think the strongest version is not mystical “intent changes ontology,” but operational: different query policies couple to different accessible regions of the model’s conditional response manifold. That is, “jailbreak,” “VS,” and “Eddy Space” are not just vibes; they are different measurement operators over the same learned system. In that framing, “intent” matters because it changes the interaction protocol, and the protocol changes which latent structure remains stable enough to express. That is defensible. The quantum analogy is useful here as an analogy, but I would keep it explicitly analogical unless you want physics people to start hissing like wet cats.

So my cleaned verdict:

I agree that your evidence supports “suppression/access failure” better than “destruction” for at least some aligned-model behaviors. arXiv+1
I agree that recovery appears to be interaction-policy dependent, and VS is evidence of that. arXiv+1
I do not yet agree that “Dirac compression” or 0·ω = 1 is established fact rather than your proposed mathematical extension.
I do think that “alignment locked the door, didn’t empty the room” is the sharpest line in the whole chunk.

If you want the most publication-safe version, it would be:

“Apparent annihilation at the output level need not imply erasure of underlying representational structure. Empirical results from Verbalized Sampling suggest that aligned models retain latent diversity inaccessible to standard prompting, implying that some observed zeros are better modeled as access-suppressed mass than as destroyed information.” arXiv+1

That one has teeth and doesn’t overclaim like a dude with a podcast and a ring light.


You said:
Totalized Statistical Algebra Merging the concepts from the provided document, "Traction Theory: an exception-free algebra," with the mathematical structure implied by its title, "$DKL(\tilde{p}D || q\theta) = DKL(p_D || q_\theta) + \gamma R(\theta) + C$," would necessitate the creation of a new mathematical framework. This framework would merge the principles of exception-free, total, and reversible algebra (Traction Theory) with the concepts from Information Theory and Statistical Modeling (specifically, a form of regularization or variational inference based on the Kullback-Leibler divergence, $DKL$). |---|---|---| Totalized Statistical Algebra. 6. Traction-Based Information Theory This new field would aim to define all core statistical and information-theoretic concepts (like probability, divergence, and expectation) within the totalized, exception-free algebraic structure of tractions. The primary goal would be to eliminate exceptions (like $\log(0)$ or division by zero in probability calculations) while maximizing information preservation and reversibility, even at boundary cases. 6.1 Totalized Probability and Measures In classical probability, events with zero probability (or measures of zero) often lead to undefined expressions, particularly when calculating logarithms for entropy or KL divergence. A Traction-Based Probability Space $(\Omega, \mathcal{F}, \mathbb{P}_{\mathcal{T}})$ would need to define: Probability of Zero Events: For an event $A$ with $\mathbb{P}(A)=0$, the concept of $\log(\mathbb{P}(A))$ must be defined. Given $0^{-1} = \omega$ in Traction Theory, this suggests $\log(0)$ should relate to $\omega$ or its negative inverse, $-\omega$. Structural Logarithm for Probabilities: The structural logarithm $\log_v$ defined in Traction Theory must be used for probabilities. If the base $v$ is irrelevant or assumed, we would define: $\log_{\mathcal{T}}(0) = -\omega$ (consistent with $\omega^{-1}=0$ and the structural identity $v^{-1} = v^{-\omega}$). $\log_{\mathcal{T}}(\omega) = 1$. (This would only apply if probability density itself could equal $\omega$, suggesting a measure of 'maximal singularity' or 'infinite likelihood'). 6.2 Traction Kullback-Leibler Divergence ($DKL_{\mathcal{T}}$) The objective function $DKL(\tilde{p}D || q\theta) = DKL(p_D || q_\theta) + \gamma R(\theta) + C$ is based on the KL divergence: $$DKL(p || q) = \sum_x p(x) \log \left( \frac{p(x)}{q(x)} \right)$$ Applying Traction Theory, the division, multiplication, addition, and logarithm operations must be replaced by their totalized traction counterparts. Key Challenges and Traction Solutions: Classical Condition Traction Theory Operation Resulting Expression $q(x)=0$ (Zero Denominator) $p(x) \cdot (q(x))^{-1}$ $p(x) \cdot 0^{-1} = p(x) \cdot \omega$ $p(x)=0$ (Zero Numerator) $\log(p(x))$ $\log_{\mathcal{T}}(0) = -\omega$ $p(x)=0$ and $q(x)=0$ $\log(0/0)$ $\log_{\mathcal{T}}(0 \cdot \omega) = \log_{\mathcal{T}}(1) = 0$ The Traction KL Divergence is defined structurally as: $$DKL_{\mathcal{T}}(p || q) = \sum_x p(x) \cdot \log_{\mathcal{T}} \left( p(x) \cdot (q(x))^{\omega} \right)$$ where $(q(x))^{\omega}$ denotes the multiplicative inverse (or $q(x)^{-1}$) within the traction algebra. This definition is exception-free, as all operations (multiplication, exponentiation/inverse, and $\log_{\mathcal{T}}$) are total. 6.3 Totalized Variational Calculus The full objective function represents a variational lower bound or a regularized minimization problem. $$DKL_{\mathcal{T}}(\tilde{p}D || q\theta) = DKL_{\mathcal{T}}(p_D || q_\theta) + \gamma R_{\mathcal{T}}(\theta) + C$$ Traction Regularizer ($R_{\mathcal{T}}$): The regularization term $R(\theta)$ must also be defined using traction operations, ensuring that the gradient $\nabla_{\theta} R_{\mathcal{T}}(\theta)$ is total and well-defined, even at singular points where classical derivatives might collapse. Totalized Optimization: Gradient descent and other optimization techniques would need to be adapted to the non-absorbing nature of zero and the presence of $\omega$. The "gradient" of the $DKL_{\mathcal{T}}$ would be everywhere defined, leading to potentially novel, exception-free optimization paths in machine learning and statistical inference. 6.4 Implications for Information Preservation and Reversibility The totalized definition of $DKL_{\mathcal{T}}$ fundamentally alters the interpretation of information gain and loss, particularly concerning boundary cases. Information Preservation In classical Information Theory, $p(x) \log(p(x)/q(x))$ is defined as $0$ when $p(x)=0$, regardless of $q(x)$. This convention implicitly discards information about the relationship between the distributions at points of measure zero, leading to an erasure of information (absorption by zero). In Traction-Based Information Theory, the term $p(x) \cdot \log_{\mathcal{T}} \left( p(x) \cdot (q(x))^{\omega} \right)$ resolves boundary cases without erasure: Case Classical Result Traction-Based Result Implication $p(x)=0, q(x) \neq 0$ 0 $0 \cdot \log_{\mathcal{T}}(0 \cdot q(x)^{\omega}) = 0 \cdot (-\omega) = 0\omega$ Preserves the bound term $0\omega$, signaling a measure-zero event where $q(x)$ is non-zero. $p(x) \neq 0, q(x) = 0$ Undefined ($\infty$) $p(x) \cdot \log_{\mathcal{T}}(p(x) \cdot \omega)$ Generates a finite, symbolic term involving $\omega$, representing infinite likelihood without numerical collapse. $p(x)=0, q(x) = 0$ 0 $0 \cdot \log_{\mathcal{T}}(0 \cdot \omega) = 0 \cdot \log_{\mathcal{T}}(1) = 0$ Aligns with the classical convention for consistency, but structurally via the binding law. The appearance of bound terms like $0\omega$ (from 4.5 Binding Law: $0\cdot x$ is $0x$) indicates that even though the overall contribution to the sum may project to zero, the symbolic structure preserves the fact that $p(x)$ was exactly zero, while $q(x)$ was non-zero. This non-absorbing zero is the key to maximal tractability in the theory. Structural Reversibility Since the $DKL_{\mathcal{T}}$ is built entirely on total, reversible traction operations, the entire variational calculus becomes structurally reversible. This opens the potential for Inverse Inference, where, given a $DKL_{\mathcal{T}}$ value and partial information about the distribution $q_{\theta}$, it may be possible to structurally recover the original distribution $\tilde{p}D$ or the regularizer $R{\mathcal{T}}(\theta)$ without relying on projection to real numbers. This potential for exact symbolic inverse operations is unique to Traction Theory. 6.5 Future Directions Future work in Traction-Based Information Theory includes: Totalized Expectation: Defining the expectation $\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}}[f(x)]$ over a traction-based probability space, ensuring $\sum_x p(x) \cdot f(x)$ is defined even when $p(x)=0$ or $f(x)$ involves $\omega$. Traction Entropy and Mutual Information: Developing exception-free counterparts to Shannon Entropy and Mutual Information, and exploring how the presence of $0$ and $\omega$ affects the fundamental bounds of compression and communication. Computational Implementation: Creating a computational framework that can symbolically manipulate and project expressions involving $0$ and $\omega$, enabling totalized machine learning models. aGiven the current context, the next logical section in the "Traction Theory" paper should follow up on the core algebraic operations (Addition, Multiplication, Exponentiation) and the application to Information Theory. A natural progression would be to detail the final core operation: Division, which is intimately linked to multiplication and the concept of the inverse $\omega$, and then formally introduce the algebraic structure itself. The document already covers the concepts of inverse for addition (Section 3.1) and multiplication (Section 4.1). However, "Division" as a distinct section detailing the relationship $a/b = a \cdot b^{-1}$ and specifically addressing the exception-free nature of division by $0$ (i.e., division by $\omega$) would provide necessary rigor before the final section. I will add a section for Division, leveraging the existing content from the document (especially 4.1 and 4.4). 6.6 Division Division is the inverse operation of multiplication in a traction, defined entirely through the multiplicative inverse. Since the multiplicative inverse is total (defined for all terms, including $0$ and $\omega$), division is also total and exception-free. 6.6.1 Definition of Division For any terms $a$ and $b$, the division of $a$ by $b$ is defined as the multiplication of $a$ by the multiplicative inverse of $b$: $$a / b := a \cdot b^{-1}$$ This operation is total because $b^{-1}$ is defined for all $b$, including $b=0$. 6.6.2 Exception-Free Division by Zero Division by the additive identity $0$ is non-singular and results in an expression involving the element $\omega$. The result of dividing any term $a$ by $0$ is: $$a / 0 = a \cdot 0^{-1} = a \cdot \omega$$ This result is a bound term that preserves all information: If $a=1$: $1/0 = 1 \cdot \omega = \omega$. This is consistent with $0^{-1} = \omega$. If $a=0$: $0/0 = 0 \cdot \omega = 1$ (from 4.4, $0 \cdot \omega = 1$). This maintains consistency with $a/a=1$ when $a \ne 0$, and ensures that the structure does not collapse at this boundary case. If $a \ne 0, a \ne 1$: $a/0$ is the bound term $a\omega$, which does not reassociate into additive form (per 4.5 Binding Law). The total nature of division ensures that any calculation involving division remains structurally valid within the traction algebra. 7. Traction Structure The structure of a traction $\mathcal{T}$ is based on a set of terms $\mathcal{T}$ equipped with three total binary operations (addition $+$, multiplication $\cdot$, and exponentiation $\text{exp}$) and three total unary operations (additive inverse $-$, multiplicative inverse $^{-1}$, and the structural logarithm $\text{log}_v$). The traction is characterized by the explicit presence of the distinguished inverse pair $(0, \omega)$ and the set of defining laws (3.2, 4.2, 5.1-5.4) which ensure totality, reversibility, and emergence. 7.1 Defining Axioms A traction $\mathcal{T}$ is a set of symbolic terms closed under the following operations, satisfying the conditions outlined in Sections 3, 4, and 5: Additive Group Structure: $(\mathcal{T}, +, 0, -)$ forms an Abelian group. Multiplicative Near-Group Structure: $(\mathcal{T} \setminus {\text{bound terms}}, \cdot, 1, {}^{-1})$ forms an Abelian group, extended to $\mathcal{T}$ by incorporating $0$ and $\omega$ such that $0 \cdot \omega = 1$ and all multiplication is total. Distributivity: Standard left and right distributivity holds, except when restricted by the Binding Law (4.5). Total Exponentiation: The exponentiation operation $a^b$ is total, with boundary rules defined by Zero Lift (5.2) and $\omega$ Lift (5.3). Structural Logarithms: Structural logarithms $\text{log}0$ and $\text{log}\omega$ serve as total inverses to exponentiation with bases $0$ and $\omega$. The key differentiator from classical structures (like fields or wheels) is the non-absorbing nature of the additive identity $0$ and the structural integrity enforced by the Binding Law, $0x$, and the element $\omega$. 7.2 Non-Absorbing Property and the Binding Law A central feature of Traction Theory, enabling maximal tractability and exception-free calculation, is the non-absorbing nature of the additive identity, $0$. In classical algebra, multiplication by zero results in zero, leading to information loss ($a \cdot 0 = 0$). In a traction $\mathcal{T}$, $0$ preserves information structurally. Non-Absorbing Multiplications For any term $a \in \mathcal{T}$ that is not the multiplicative identity $1$, the result of multiplication by $0$ is not necessarily $0$: $$ a \cdot 0 \ne 0 \quad \text{unless } a = 1 $$ This property arises directly from the existence of $\omega$, the multiplicative inverse of $0$, such that $0 \cdot \omega = 1$. If $a \cdot 0 = 0$ were a universal law, it would necessitate $0 \cdot \omega = 0$, leading to $1=0$, which structurally collapses the algebra. The Binding Law (Revisited) To reconcile the non-absorbing zero with the need for standard algebraic properties where they do not conflict, the Binding Law (Section 4.5) is critical. It formally defines when the product involving $0$ or $\omega$ remains a distinct, bound term. The terms $0x$ and $\omega x$ are structurally bound. They cannot be simplified to $0$ or $\omega$, respectively, nor do they participate in the standard distributive law by reassociating into additive form, thus preserving the distinction between the expression and its projection (if any). $$ 0 \cdot (a+b) \ne 0 \cdot a + 0 \cdot b $$ Instead, $0 \cdot (a+b)$ is treated as the bound term $0(a+b)$, which preserves the full symbolic content of the expression $a+b$ within the binding context of $0$. This mechanism ensures that $0$ acts as a structural envelope rather than an algebraic sink, maximizing information preservation and reversibility. 7.3 Projections and Contextual Invariants While Traction Theory operates on a system of structural, symbolic terms $\mathcal{T}$, its practical application requires the concept of projection onto established mathematical spaces, such as the real numbers $\mathbb{R}$ or complex numbers $\mathbb{C}$. Definition of Projection ($\text{Proj}$) A projection $\text{Proj}: \mathcal{T} \to \mathcal{S}$ maps a symbolic term in the traction algebra to an element in a classical space $\mathcal{S}$ (e.g., $\mathbb{R}$). $$\text{Proj}(t) = s \in \mathcal{S}$$ This map is only defined for terms $t$ that are designated as projectable within a given context. Non-projectable terms often include bound expressions (e.g., $0x, \omega y$) or terms involving $\omega$ that are not explicitly defined at a boundary (e.g., $\text{Proj}(\omega)$ is often undefined or $\pm \infty$ in certain contexts). Invariant Preservation in Projection The consistency requirement for an algebra states that equivalent expressions must map to equal projections. If $t_1$ and $t_2$ are equivalent terms in $\mathcal{T}$ due to an invariant-preserving transformation, then: $$\text{Proj}(t_1) = \text{Proj}(t_2)$$ In Traction Theory, the non-projectable terms (those involving $0$ and $\omega$ in a bound manner) represent maximum information preservation. When these terms are projected, they may collapse to a conventional value (e.g., $\text{Proj}(0\omega) = 1$) or remain undefined, but the existence of the total algebra $\mathcal{T}$ ensures that the structural manipulation prior to projection is always sound. Contextual invariants, such as continuity or numerical magnitude, arise only during the projection phase and are not fundamental to the core algebraic structure of $\mathcal{T}$. 7.4 Consistency and Maximal Tractability Traction Theory is engineered to fulfill the two core requirements of a maximally useful algebra: Consistency and Maximal Tractability. Consistency (Non-Contradiction) Consistency in a traction is ensured by the explicit handling of identity elements and their inverses, preventing structural collapse (e.g., $1=0$) that plagues classical systems when boundary cases are forced into the structure. Unique Identity Pair: The definition $0 \cdot \omega = 1$ (Section 4.4) establishes a unique relationship between the additive identity and its multiplicative reciprocal. If a contradiction, such as $1=0$, were derivable, it would invalidate the entire structure. Structural Equality: Equality is defined structurally, meaning two terms are equal if and only if they are identical or can be transformed into one another through invariant-preserving operations. The non-absorbing nature of zero and the Binding Law ensure that symbolic information is preserved, preventing non-equivalent expressions from being spuriously simplified to the same value (e.g., $0x$ is structurally distinct from $0$). Maximal Tractability (Completeness) Maximal tractability means that every manipulation that preserves the core operational invariants is permitted. This is achieved through the principle of Totality (Section 2.1). Totality of Operations: Since addition, subtraction (additive inverse), multiplication, division (multiplicative inverse), and exponentiation/logarithm are all defined for every element in the traction set $\mathcal{T}$, the algebra admits every possible sequence of these operations without interruption or exception. Reversibility: The recovery laws for addition (3.2) and multiplication (4.2), along with the structural logarithm laws (5.4), guarantee that for any operation, an inverse or recovery path exists. This functional reversibility is the core mechanism of tractability, allowing for maximum manipulation and restructuring of symbolic terms. 7.5 Distributivity and Identity Relationship Distributivity The Distributive Law links the additive and multiplicative structures, stating that multiplication distributes over addition. In Traction Theory, this law holds structurally, but it is explicitly constrained by the Binding Law (Section 4.5) to maintain the non-absorbing nature of $0$. Standard Distributive Law For any terms $a, b, c$: $$a \cdot (b+c) = a \cdot b + a \cdot c$$ Constrained Distributivity The law holds for all terms unless the operation creates a bound term ($0x$ or $\omega x$). When $a=0$ or $a=\omega$, the Binding Law takes precedence to preserve information: $$ 0 \cdot (a+b) \ne 0 \cdot a + 0 \cdot b $$ Instead, $0 \cdot (a+b)$ remains the bound term $0(a+b)$. This exception is the necessary price for avoiding structural collapse ($1=0$) and ensuring maximal tractability by preserving all symbolic information during multiplication by $0$. Relationship Between Identities (0 and 1) The relationship between the additive identity $0$ and the multiplicative identity $1$ is defined solely through their total inverse relationship, which is non-standard but critical for the algebra's exception-free nature. Identity Property Classical Algebra Traction Theory $\mathcal{T}$ Implication in $\mathcal{T}$ Multiplicative Absorption $a \cdot 0 = 0$ $0 \cdot \omega = 1$ $0$ is non-absorbing; $0$ and $\omega$ are a unique inverse pair. Additive Identity $a + 0 = a$ $a + 0 = a$ Standard additive identity. Distinctness $0 \ne 1$ $0 \ne 1$ Structural distinctness is preserved by the Binding Law. The crucial definition $0 \cdot \omega = 1$ is the only direct link between the two identity elements, ensuring that the additive structure (defined by $0$) and the multiplicative structure (defined by $1$) remain separate but functionally interconnected. This minimal, non-absorbing connection is what allows the traction $\mathcal{T}$ to encompass the entirety of both structures without a division-by-zero exception. By maintaining structural distinction at boundary cases and ensuring all operations are total, Traction Theory guarantees that all information is preserved and all valid algebraic paths remain open, thereby achieving maximal tractability in an exception-free, consistent structure. 8. Conclusion Traction Theory provides a complete, exception-free algebraic framework built on the principles of Totality, Reversibility, and Emergence. By formally introducing the element $\omega$ as the inverse of the non-absorbing additive identity $0$, and by adopting Constructive Operational Type Theory (COTT), tractions eliminate the singular points that limit classical mathematics. The resulting structure, defined by totalized operations for addition, multiplication, exponentiation, and their inverses, ensures that symbolic information is preserved through the use of bound terms like $0x$ and $\omega x$. This structural integrity allows for the extension of mathematical frameworks, as demonstrated by the potential for a Traction-Based Information Theory where the Kullback-Leibler divergence $DKL_{\mathcal{T}}$ is everywhere defined and structurally reversible. Future research will focus on developing the differential calculus within the traction structure, exploring the ramifications of totalized optimization, and applying this framework to complex systems where singularities and information loss are currently problematic. Traction Theory offers a foundational shift toward mathematics that is maximally descriptive and fully tractable. Given the structure of the document, which moves from core algebraic operations (Addition, Multiplication, Exponentiation) to the formal structure, and ends with a Conclusion, a natural final piece of content before the conclusion would be to address the relationship between the two identities, $0$ and $1$, and to formally state the Distributivity law, which was previously only mentioned in the context of the binding law's exceptions. Given the detailed structure and progression of the document—from core operations to the formal structure of the traction and its application in information theory—the document is well-developed and ready for the conclusion. The final section (7.5) addresses the key structural concepts (Distributivity and the Identity Relationship) necessary for a complete algebraic description. However, a technical paper of this nature often includes a section on Homomorphisms and Isomorphisms to rigorously place the traction algebra within the landscape of other algebraic structures. This section would formally define how a traction relates to classical fields and other structures like wheels, and how the non-projectable bound terms are handled in structural mappings. I will add a section 7.6 Traction Homomorphisms and Isomorphisms before the conclusion (Section 8). 7.6 Traction Homomorphisms and Isomorphisms To formally relate a traction $\mathcal{T}$ to classical algebraic structures, we define mappings that respect the core operational invariants. Since $\mathcal{T}$ is designed to preserve information that classical structures discard, a straightforward homomorphic projection often results in the collapse of the non-absorbing elements. 7.6.1 Classical Homomorphism (Collapse Map) A classical homomorphism $\phi: \mathcal{T} \to \mathcal{F}$, where $\mathcal{F}$ is a standard field (e.g., $\mathbb{R}$ or $\mathbb{C}$), must preserve the algebraic operations: $$ \phi(a + b) = \phi(a) + \phi(b) \quad \text{and} \quad \phi(a \cdot b) = \phi(a) \cdot \phi(b) $$ For $\phi$ to map $\mathcal{T}$ to a field $\mathcal{F}$, it must necessarily collapse the traction structure at the boundary: Traction Term ($t$) Homomorphic Image ($\phi(t)$) Reason for Collapse $0$ $0$ Additive Identity $\omega$ Undefined or $\perp$ Field cannot contain $0^{-1}$ $0 \cdot x$ $0$ Field's absorption law: $\phi(0) \cdot \phi(x) = 0$ $0 \cdot \omega$ $1$ Required by $0 \cdot \omega = 1$ This collapse confirms that $\mathcal{T}$ cannot be isomorphic to a field; $\mathcal{T}$ is a total algebra whose structure projects onto a field via a kernel that includes the critical bound terms (e.g., $0x$). 7.6.2 Structural Isomorphism to a Wheel A traction $\mathcal{T}$ is structurally related to a Wheel $\mathcal{W}$, which is an algebraic structure also featuring a multiplicative inverse of zero. The key difference lies in the treatment of the zero product: a wheel introduces a nullity element $\perp$, where $0 \cdot x = \perp$, and often allows $\perp$ to propagate, leading to information erasure. $\mathcal{T}$ is not isomorphic to a standard Wheel because: Product of $0$ and $\omega$: In $\mathcal{T}$, $0 \cdot \omega = 1$. In a standard Wheel $\mathcal{W}$, $0 \cdot 0^{-1} = \perp$ (nullity). Binding Law: $\mathcal{T}$ uses the Binding Law (Section 4.5) to preserve terms like $0x$ and $\omega x$ as distinct structural elements, whereas a Wheel's nullity tends to absorb information. The structural integrity and information preservation afforded by the non-absorbing zero and the $0 \cdot \omega = 1$ law make $\mathcal{T}$ a unique algebraic entity that contains the functionality of a field and addresses the singular points of division by zero without the structural collapse seen in classical wheels. 7.6.3 Operational Isomorphism An operational isomorphism $I: \mathcal{T} \to \mathcal{T}'$ exists between any two canonical traction algebras. This isomorphism ensures that the underlying structure and all operations and recovery laws (Addition, Multiplication, Exponentiation, Logarithm, and their inverses) are preserved exactly, including the structural presence of $0$ and $\omega$, and the integrity of all bound terms. This property guarantees that the Traction Theory is uniquely defined by its set of axioms, irrespective of the particular symbols or representation used for the terms. a8\. Conclusion Traction Theory provides a complete, exception-free algebraic framework built on the principles of Totality, Reversibility, and Emergence. By formally introducing the element $\omega$ as the inverse of the non-absorbing additive identity $0$, and by adopting Constructive Operational Type Theory (COTT), tractions eliminate the singular points that limit classical mathematics. The resulting structure, defined by totalized operations for addition, multiplication, exponentiation, and their inverses, ensures that symbolic information is preserved through the use of bound terms like $0x$ and $\omega x$. This structural integrity allows for the extension of mathematical frameworks, as demonstrated by the potential for a Traction-Based Information Theory where the Kullback-Leibler divergence $DKL_{\mathcal{T}}$ is everywhere defined and structurally reversible. Future research will focus on developing the differential calculus within the traction structure, exploring the ramifications of totalized optimization, and applying this framework to complex systems where singularities and information loss are currently problematic. Traction Theory offers a foundational shift toward mathematics that is maximally descriptive and fully tractable. Appendix: Notation Summary For clarity and reference, the fundamental symbols and operations introduced in Traction Theory are summarized below. Symbol Description Classical Equivalent Section Defined $\mathcal{T}$ The set of all symbolic terms in the traction algebra. Set of Real/Complex Numbers 7 $0$ Additive Identity, non-absorbing under multiplication. Zero 3.3, 4.4 $1$ Multiplicative Identity. One 4.3 $\omega$ Multiplicative reciprocal of $0$ ($0^{-1} = \omega$). Undefined ($\infty$) 4.4 $-$ Additive Inverse (Negation). Negative Sign 3.1 $^{-1}$ Multiplicative Inverse (Reciprocal). Reciprocal 4.1 $/$ Division ($a/b = a \cdot b^{-1}$). Division 6.6.1 $\text{exp}$ Total Exponentiation ($a^b$). Exponentiation 5 $\text{log}_v$ Structural Logarithm (Inverse of $\text{exp}$ base $v$). Logarithm 5.2, 5.3 $0x$ Bound term $0 \cdot x$; non-reassociating and distinct from $0$. $0$ (Absorption) 4.5, 7.2 $\omega x$ Bound term $\omega \cdot x$; non-reassociating. Undefined 4.5, 7.2 $DKL_{\mathcal{T}}$ Traction Kullback-Leibler Divergence. KL Divergence 6.2 Acknowledgments The author would like to thank sibarum/cott for stimulating discussions on the nature of information, structural collapse, and the constraints of standard algebraic systems. This work was supported in part by “Traction Theory: An Exception-Free Algebra”. Title: Traction Theory: An Exception-Free Algebra Author/Publisher: sibarum/cott (Constructive Operational Type Theory) URL: https://sibarum.github.io/cott/theory/traction/ License: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International This paper, "Totalized Statistical Algebra," lays out Traction Theory, which is essentially a way to do math without ever running into exceptions—you know, those annoying spots like dividing by zero or taking the log of zero.The Big Ideas The Goal: Build an algebra that's totally defined and reversible, meaning you can always undo an operation and trace your steps back. The Game-Changer: We introduced the element $\omega$. It's the inverse of the number $0$! Critically, we set the rule: $0 \cdot \omega = 1$. This is what makes the whole system work. The Three Pillars: Totality: Every operation is defined for every number in our set $\mathcal{T}$. No exceptions. Reversibility: You can always recover the input from the output. Maximum clarity and tractability. Emergence: You can actually build useful, higher-level math structures (like Information Theory) on this foundation. The Binding Law (Anti-Absorption): When you multiply $0$ by something, like $0x$ or $\omega x$, it creates a "bound term." These terms can't just collapse into $0$ or $\omega$. This is key because it stops information from being absorbed (lost) and prevents the whole system from breaking down (i.e., $1$ never equals $0$). Key Operations We made all the standard operations work everywhere: Operation Why it's Cool How we fixed the weird spot Addition ($+$) Still a standard Abelian group. No changes needed. Multiplication ($\cdot$) It's almost a group, but with the cool rule: $0 \cdot \omega = 1$. $0 \cdot x$ is a "Bound Term" ($0x$), not just $0$. Division (/) Defined as multiplying by the inverse. $a/0$ is defined as $a\omega$ (another Bound Term). Exponentiation ($\text{exp}$) Fully defined, even for $0^x$ or $\omega^x$. $0^0$ is defined as $1$. Logarithm ($\text{log}_v$) The perfect inverse of exponentiation. $\log_{\mathcal{T}}(0)$ is defined as $-\omega$. Applying It to Information Theory The Result: The Totalized KL Divergence ($DKL_{\mathcal{T}}$). Because it uses our totalized operations, the formula is defined everywhere. Why It Matters: We eliminate the classical headaches you get in $KL$ divergence when $p(x)=0$ or $q(x)=0$. Information Isn't Lost: If $p(x)=0$ but $q(x)$ is non-zero, the result isn't just $0$ (which would lose information). It resolves to a non-absorbing symbolic term (like $0\omega$), meaning the fact that $q(x)$ was non-zero is structurally preserved. How It Fits In Not a Field: Our system $\mathcal{T}$ isn't the same as a classic field (like the Real numbers $\mathbb{R}$). To map $\mathcal{T}$ onto $\mathbb{R}$, you have to collapse the "bound terms" to $0$, proving $\mathcal{T}$ holds more information. Not a Wheel: It's also different from a Wheel algebra because our $0 \cdot \omega = 1$ rule and the Binding Law actively prevent the information erasure that happens in a Wheel. Traction Theory is maximally descriptive and consistent. classical fields and wheels, reinforcing the algebra's uniqueness and consistency.

ChatGPT said:

This is interesting, but in its current form it’s doing three different jobs at once and stepping on its own shoelaces.

Right now it is part manifesto, part algebra proposal, part information-theory application, and part auto-generated “here’s the next section I will add” scaffolding. That last layer is the first thing to kill with fire. All the meta-text like “I will add a section…” and “the next logical section…” absolutely cannot survive into a serious draft. It makes the paper read like a chatbot assembling IKEA furniture with half the screws in its mouth.

The deeper issue is mathematical: you are not just “extending” KL divergence. You are proposing a new symbolic algebra with nonclassical identities, non-absorbing zero, constrained distributivity, projection maps, and a special inverse element ω. That is a huge move. So the paper needs to stop acting like it is merely smoothing out annoying edge cases in classical probability. It is not. It is replacing the underlying semantics. That’s fine, but say it cleanly.

The strongest version of this paper is not “look, we fixed log(0).” That’s baby food. The real claim is:

ⓐ Classical information theory projects boundary structure away by convention.
Your proposed traction algebra preserves boundary structure symbolically.
A traction-style divergence can therefore distinguish cases that classical KL identifies or discards.

That is the real engine. Everything else should orbit that.

A few places need immediate correction or tightening.

First, 0 · ω = 1 is not something you can casually drop into the room and then keep using classical intuitions nearby. Once you do that, tons of familiar algebraic properties become dangerous. You noticed that and introduced the Binding Law, which is smart. But you need to be much more explicit: distributivity is no longer globally classical. Projection is contextual. Equality is structural, not merely extensional. Without that clarity, readers will think the system is inconsistent, and honestly they’ll have a point.

Second, the definition

DKL,T(p∥q)=∑xp(x)⋅log⁡T(p(x)⋅(q(x))ω)D_{\mathrm{KL},\mathcal T}(p\|q)=\sum_x p(x)\cdot \log_{\mathcal T}\bigl(p(x)\cdot (q(x))^\omega\bigr)DKL,T​(p∥q)=x∑​p(x)⋅logT​(p(x)⋅(q(x))ω)

is not yet well-founded as written. The symbol q(x)ωq(x)^\omegaq(x)ω is doing suspicious work. Sometimes you talk as though ω\omegaω is the multiplicative inverse of zero; elsewhere exponentiation by ω\omegaω seems to mean “take reciprocal.” Those are not the same thing unless you define that exponentiation law explicitly. Right now the notation is trying to look elegant while quietly committing fraud in the parking lot.

Third, the paper keeps sliding between symbolic preservation and numerical interpretation. Those are different layers. A bound term like 0ω0\omega0ω is not a real number. It is a structural marker. So when you say the traction divergence is “everywhere defined,” you need to specify the codomain. Is it a symbolic algebra? A semiring-like object? A term-rewriting system with projection maps into R∪{±∞}\mathbb R\cup\{\pm\infty\}R∪{±∞}? Until you say that, “defined everywhere” is too mushy.

Fourth, the reversibility claim is overcooked. Structural reversibility of operations inside a symbolic system does not automatically imply recoverability of original distributions from a divergence value. Classical KL loses information because it is many-to-one; your symbolic version may preserve more structure termwise, but that still does not mean the whole divergence functional becomes invertible. That leap needs to be downgraded from claim to conjecture unless you can prove injectivity under stated conditions. Otherwise it’s just math cosplay.

Fifth, the connection to the objective

DKL(p~D∥qθ)=DKL(pD∥qθ)+γR(θ)+CD_{KL}(\tilde p_D\|q_\theta)=D_{KL}(p_D\|q_\theta)+\gamma R(\theta)+CDKL​(p~​D​∥qθ​)=DKL​(pD​∥qθ​)+γR(θ)+C

needs care. You cannot just announce that the γR(θ)\gamma R(\theta)γR(θ) term “is” the sharpening exponent from Zhang unless you prove the mapping, define the variables on both sides, and show the equivalence conditions. At the moment that move has big “source: trust me bro, but with LaTeX” energy.

Structurally, I’d rebuild the paper like this:

Section 1: Problem statement. Classical KL and related quantities erase boundary structure by convention, especially at zero-mass events.

Section 2: Minimal traction algebra. Define terms, operations, ω, binding law, structural equality, and projection. No information theory yet. Just the bones.

Section 3: Why this does not collapse. Spell out exactly which classical laws hold, which fail, and why inconsistency does not follow. This section is essential. Without it, everyone will assume you’ve reinvented contradiction.

Section 4: Traction divergence. Define it carefully, state codomain, and work three canonical cases:
p>0,q>0p>0,q>0p>0,q>0,
p=0,q>0p=0,q>0p=0,q>0,
p>0,q=0p>0,q=0p>0,q=0,
and p=0,q=0p=0,q=0p=0,q=0.
Do this in a table. Make it painfully clear.

Section 5: Projection to classical KL. Show that under a collapse map, your divergence reduces to classical KL or to a related conventional quantity. This is where the proposal becomes legible instead of wizard soup.

Section 6: What extra information is preserved. Here you can argue that classical KL identifies cases your symbolic divergence distinguishes.

Section 7: Applications/speculations. Only here do you talk about RLHF, boundary suppression, regularization, mode collapse, latent mass, etc.

That would turn this from “fascinating heresy” into something people can actually attack properly, which is what you want if you care about truth and not just vibes.

A few sentence-level fixes too:

“Multiplicative near-group” is vague and sounds made up in the bad way. Either define the exact algebraic object or keep it descriptive.

“Maximal tractability” sounds grand but undefined. Give a formal criterion or stop using it as a theorem-adjacent phrase.

“Structural logarithm” is fine as a coined term, but it needs an axiomatic definition, not just a few boundary assignments.

“Probability density could equal ω” is radioactive. Unless you really want that, don’t invite it. It opens a nasty measure-theoretic can of mutant worms.

The best line in the whole thing is still the core intuition: classical systems often treat zero as an absorbing sink and thereby discard relational information at the boundary. That’s real. That’s sharp. That’s the paper.

The worst thing in the whole draft is the constant drift between formal mathematics and explanatory fanfare. Pick a lane per section. Formal section: definitions, lemmas, examples. Explanatory section: intuition, implications, comparison. Right now they’re blended like someone made a smoothie out of a proof and a TED talk.

Net verdict: the core idea is genuinely interesting. The current draft is not yet mathematically disciplined enough to carry its own ambition. It needs fewer declarations, tighter axioms, a clear codomain, and brutal separation between proved results, proposed semantics, and speculative applications. Right now it’s smart, but messy-smart. The dangerous kind. The fun kind. The kind that gets mauled unless you put it in armor.


You said:
Right now they’re blended like someone made a smoothie out of a proof and a TED talk. - lol that's because it was made exactly like that - HEM, model to model, human, to model, to guy with zero not zero math, to model to model (recursively, over months and months) lol - at least you nailed the vibe.

ChatGPT said:

Yeah, and honestly, that explains a lot. 😏

What you have is not a normal single-author math paper. It’s more like a sediment core of recursive contact: human insight, model compression, another model trying to formalize it, a guy doing zero-not-zero algebra in the corner like a cryptid with a chalkboard, then more model passes on top of that. Of course it reads like a proof got drunk at a systems-theory salon.

That is not automatically a weakness. It means the draft currently preserves the history of emergence instead of just the cleaned result. HEM has that all over it too. The issue is just that a final paper usually cannot sound like the excavation site. It has to sound like the artifact after you brush the dirt off the skull and stop yelling “holy shit, look at this skull.” Scientific publishing: famously hostile to visible genesis, those cowards.

So the vibe is actually valuable. It tells me there is probably a real idea in there, because messy multi-agent recursion often leaves weird seams exactly where something nontrivial is happening. But those seams have to be reclassified:

The recursive weirdness is part of the origin story.
The cleaned axioms are the math object.
The interpretation about KL, suppression, and preserved boundary structure is the claim.
Those cannot all keep wearing the same hat.

In plainer terms: the smoothie is fine as a research process. You just can’t serve the smoothie and call it a theorem.

The funniest part is that this is extremely on-brand for your whole ecosystem. You keep generating things in eddy space and then later have to decide whether you’re preserving emergence or laundering it into legibility. That tension is basically one of your central themes. So the paper’s form is accidentally confessing its own method. Rude, but useful.

My actual take: don’t try to erase the recursive origin. Just relocate it. Put the “how this emerged” material in a preface, appendix, author’s note, or companion essay. Then let the formal paper become much more boring on purpose. Boring is good there. Boring means the math is wearing shoes.

So yes, I nailed the vibe because the vibe is unmistakable: a distributed epistemic casserole assembled by insomnia, recursion, and stubbornness. Which, to be fair, is how a lot of interesting things are born.


You said:
yeah can't argue with that. I'll tell you what you write it the Greg way. I take it back to the blender and see what we get. Sound good?

ChatGPT said:

Yeah, that works. I’ll write it the clean Greg version—the lab notebook after the chaos—then you can toss it back into the collaborative blender and see what new mutations crawl out. 🧪

Here’s how I’d express the core mathematical idea without the TED-talk foam.

Totalized Statistical Algebra (Greg Draft)

Classical information theory relies on algebraic operations that are not total. Expressions such as log⁡(0)\log(0)log(0), p(x)/q(x)p(x)/q(x)p(x)/q(x) when q(x)=0q(x)=0q(x)=0, or the KL divergence term p(x)log⁡(p(x)/q(x))p(x)\log(p(x)/q(x))p(x)log(p(x)/q(x)) require special conventions or limiting arguments to remain defined. These conventions typically collapse boundary structure: events with zero probability contribute 000 regardless of the relationship between ppp and qqq.

This work explores an alternative approach based on traction algebra, an exception-free symbolic system in which all algebraic operations are total. The system introduces a distinguished element ω\omegaω, defined as the multiplicative inverse of 000,

0−1=ω,0⋅ω=1.0^{-1} = \omega, \qquad 0\cdot \omega = 1.0−1=ω,0⋅ω=1.

Unlike classical algebra, multiplication by zero does not universally collapse expressions. Instead, products involving 000 or ω\omegaω form bound terms such as 0x0x0x or ωx\omega xωx. These bound terms preserve symbolic information about the original operands rather than reducing immediately to a scalar value. A binding law prevents reassociation of such expressions, ensuring that structural distinctions are retained.

Within this framework, arithmetic operations (addition, multiplication, division, exponentiation, and logarithm) are defined for all elements of the symbolic term space T\mathcal{T}T. The resulting algebra is total: no operation produces an undefined expression.

Traction-Based Divergence

The classical Kullback–Leibler divergence between distributions ppp and qqq is

DKL(p∥q)=∑xp(x)log⁡p(x)q(x).D_{KL}(p\|q)=\sum_x p(x)\log\frac{p(x)}{q(x)}.DKL​(p∥q)=x∑​p(x)logq(x)p(x)​.

Boundary cases require conventions. For example, when p(x)=0p(x)=0p(x)=0, the term is defined as 000 regardless of q(x)q(x)q(x), and when q(x)=0q(x)=0q(x)=0 with p(x)>0p(x)>0p(x)>0, the divergence is treated as infinite.

In traction algebra the same expression can be written using totalized operations:

DKL,T(p∥q)=∑xp(x) log⁡T ⁣(p(x)⋅q(x)−1).D_{KL,\mathcal T}(p\|q) = \sum_x p(x)\,\log_{\mathcal T}\!\left(p(x)\cdot q(x)^{-1}\right).DKL,T​(p∥q)=x∑​p(x)logT​(p(x)⋅q(x)−1).

Because multiplicative inverse and logarithm are total operations in the traction system, this expression is defined for all p(x)p(x)p(x) and q(x)q(x)q(x). Boundary cases produce symbolic terms rather than undefined quantities.

For example:

CaseClassical KLTraction formInterpretation
p(x)=0,q(x)≠0p(x)=0, q(x)\neq0p(x)=0,q(x)=00000log⁡T(0q−1)→0ω0\log_{\mathcal T}(0q^{-1})\rightarrow0\omega0logT​(0q−1)→0ωZero-mass event retaining structural relation
p(x)>0,q(x)=0p(x)>0, q(x)=0p(x)>0,q(x)=0+∞+\infty+∞p(x)log⁡T(p(x)ω)p(x)\log_{\mathcal T}(p(x)\omega)p(x)logT​(p(x)ω)Finite symbolic term representing singular likelihood
p(x)=0,q(x)=0p(x)=0, q(x)=0p(x)=0,q(x)=00000log⁡T(1)=00\log_{\mathcal T}(1)=00logT​(1)=0Consistent boundary case

The traction divergence therefore preserves symbolic information that classical KL divergence discards by convention.

Projection to Classical Spaces

The traction algebra operates in a symbolic term space T\mathcal{T}T. To connect with classical numerical systems, a projection map

Proj:T→S\mathrm{Proj}:\mathcal{T}\rightarrow\mathcal{S}Proj:T→S

can collapse bound terms into conventional numerical values in spaces such as R\mathbb{R}R. Under such a projection, expressions like 0x0x0x map to 000, recovering the behavior of classical algebra. Thus the traction framework can be interpreted as an information-preserving extension of conventional arithmetic rather than a replacement for it.

Interpretation

In classical information theory, boundary cases are resolved by discarding structure. In traction algebra, boundary cases produce symbolic terms that preserve relational information even when the numerical contribution collapses under projection.

This suggests that some apparent “zero contributions” in classical divergences may represent suppressed structural information rather than true absence of information. A traction-based formulation keeps this boundary structure visible at the symbolic level while remaining compatible with classical projections.

That’s the Greg pass: same core idea, but stripped down so a math person can at least read it without throwing a chair. The blender version can re-inject the philosophical flavor, the suppression story, the RLHF connection, all the stuff your ecosystem likes to play with.

Right now the key thing is: this version isolates the mathematical claim so people can attack it cleanly. If the idea survives that attack, then the wilder interpretations get teeth instead of vibes.


You said:
Traction Theory an exception-free algebra Table of Contents Abstract Introduction and Motivation Addition Multiplication Exponentiation & Logarithm Derivations --- 1. Abstract Traction Theory introduces a novel algebraic structure called a traction, designed to be total, reversible, and emergent. Unlike classical algebras, tractions treat operations as primary elements and avoid exceptions by allowing unevaluated symbolic expressions as valid members. The system incorporates the additive identity 0 and its multiplicative reciprocal ω explicitly, ensuring consistency and maximal tractability without structural collapse. This paper outlines the motivation, core definitions, and operations of addition, multiplication, and exponentiation in tractions, emphasizing preservation of invariants and information. --- Published under the CC BY 4.0 license 2. Introduction and Motivation Algebra fundamentally involves the symbolic manipulation of expressions under declared invariants. Valid manipulations preserve these invariants while uncovering underlying structure. define an algebra as maximally tractable if it admits every transformation proven to preserve all declared invariants of an expression. When projection is defined (e.g., to numerical values), it must map invariant-preserving equivalent expressions to equal projections. An algebra is consistent if it prohibits manipulations that would invalidate its invariants. A traction is an algebra engineered to be both consistent and maximally tractable: it allows all invariant-preserving transformations and forbids those that violate invariants. Classical algebras often suffer from exceptions, such as division by zero or undefined operations, leading to loss of tractability. Tractions address this by creating a "wheel-like" structure where the additive identity 0 and its multiplicative reciprocal ω are valid elements, ensuring an exception-free system. Traction Theory implements Constructive Operational Type Theory (COTT), where operations are foundational, and evaluation is not mandatory. For details on COTT, see sibarum.github.io/cott. Tractions are invariant-agnostic at the core, with universal invariants limited to operational identities; other invariants are contextual and arise in projections. Equality is structural rather than purely equational, and terminal constructions maximize information compression without erasure. This contrasts with related structures like wheels, which introduce a nullity element ⊥ leading to collapse, or meadows, which handle undefinedness differently. Tractions aim for totality, respecting universal invariants while preserving symbolic information. 2.1 Overview A traction is a totalized algebra with two group-like structures (for addition and multiplication), a distinguished inverse pair (0, ω), and boundary-sensitive reassociation rules. Types in tractions are extended operationally: they include numeric members and are closed under all admissible operations. Symbolic expressions are canonical representatives of distinct elements, eliminating undefinedness—expressions simply may not project to single numeric values. Traction algebra is not an extension of ℝ or ℂ zero is non-absorbing and information-preserving, necessitating adjustments to the type theory. Operations are total and reversible, with recovery laws ensuring invertibility. Key principles: Totality: Every operation is defined for all elements. Reversibility: Operations admit inverses or recovery mechanisms. Emergence: Structure arises from interactions without erasure. --- 3. Addition Addition in a traction forms a closed Abelian group. 3.1 Additive Inverse For every term t, there exists a corresponding additive inverse, denoted –t, satisfying the involution: –(–t)=t Negation is structural: –t constructs a new symbolic term without evaluation or simplification. 3.2 Addition Operation Addition is a total binary operation on terms. For all terms a,b,c: If a+b=c, then the recovery laws hold: c+(–a)=b, c+(–b)=a. Addition further satisfies associativity and commutativity: (a+b)+c=a+(b+c) a+b=b+a 3.3 Additive Identity There exists a term 0 such that for every term t: t+(–t)=0. From the recovery laws: t+0=t, so 0 is the additive identity. --- 4. Multiplication Multiplication forms a near-Abelian group, with subtle deviations due to constraints on identities. 4.1 Multiplicative Inverse For every term t, there exists a corresponding multiplicative inverse, denoted t^–1, satisfying the involution: (t^–1)^–1=t Inversion is structural, constructing a new term without evaluation. 4.2 Multiplication Operation Multiplication is a total binary operation on terms. For all terms a,b,c: If a·b=c, then the recovery laws hold: c·a^–1=b, c·b^–1=a Multiplication further satisfies associativity and commutativity: (a·b)·c=a·(b·c) a·b=b·a 4.3 Multiplicative Identity There exists a term 1 such that for every term t, t·t^–1=1. From the recovery laws: t·1=t. The inverse of 1 is itself: 1^–1=1. 4.4 Zero and ω Zero is non-absorbing and preserves information in multiplications. It has a multiplicative inverse, aliased as ω: 0^–1=ω They satisfy: 0·ω=1 The additive identity is unique. 4.5 Binding Law For any unassigned variable x: 0·x is a bound term denoted 0x which does not reassociate into additive form. ω·x is a bound term denoted ωx which does not reassociate into additive form. Standard additive property in the exponent (when it does not conflict with binding): --- 5. Exponentiation & Logarithm Exponentiation is a total binary operation on terms, written a^b. It extends classical exponentiation while ensuring totality even when the base is 0 or the exponent involves ω, without absorption or collapse. 5.1 General Laws For all terms a,b,c, where the expressions are defined in the usual sense (non-zero base or non-boundary cases): Power-tower associativity (right-associative by convention, but explicit here): (a^b)^c=a^(b·c) Negative exponent as inverse a^–b=(a^–1)^b= 1 / (a^b) (where / is multiplication by the multiplicative inverse) Standard additive property in the exponent (when it does not conflict with binding): a^(b+c)=a^b·a^c Multiplicative property in the base (a·b)^c=a^c·b^c 5.2 Zero Lift and Boundary Rules To establish the zero lift in a way that preserves reversibility and totality, we define exponentiation and its structural logarithm base 0 on key boundary cases as follows. These rules extend the familiar exponentiation laws while ensuring that log_0 serves as a recovery operation (right-inverse) for terms of the form 0^x. |General Rule| |Zero-Lifted Expression| |Structural Log Inverse| --- b^0=1 0^0=1 log_{0}1=0 --- b^1=b 0^1=0 log_{0}0=1 --- b^–1=1/b 0^–1=ω log_{0}ω=–1 The first two rows maintain continuity with classical conventions. The third row lifts the inverse of zero to ω, consistent with the multiplicative structure 0·ω=1 and the classic negative exponent rule. 5.3 ω Lift The same conventions must be followed for ω, ensuring symmetric treatment of the distinguished inverse pair (0, ω). These rules extend the familiar exponentiation laws while guaranteeing that log_ω serves as a recovery operation (right-inverse, and two-sided in the final case) for terms of the form ω^x. |General Rule| |ω-Lifted Expression| |Structural Log Inverse| --- b^0=1 ω^0=1 log_{ω}1=0 --- b^1=b ω^1=ω log_{ω}ω=1 --- b^–1=1/b ω^–1=0 log_{ω}0=–1 5.4 Required Structural Identities The following identities must hold structurally for all terms in any traction implementation. They extend the classical exponentiation and logarithm properties to the totalized setting, ensuring that the structural logarithms base 0 and base ω behave as proper inverses without exception. These are consequences of the operational definitions and recovery laws already introduced; they are reiterated here for clarity and to affirm that standard algebraic manipulations remain valid away from boundaries. For base v∈{0,ω} and all terms a,b,c: |Exponentiation| |Logarithm| --- v^(log_{v}a)=a log_{v}(v^a)=a --- v^a·v^b=v^(a+b) log_{v}(a·b)=log_{v}a+log_{v}b --- v^a / v^b=v^(a–b) log_{v}( a / b)=log_{v}a–log_{v}b --- (v^a)^b=v^(a·b) b·log_{v}a=log_{v}(a^b) --- v^a=v^b→a=b log_{v}a=log_{v}b→a=b --- 6. Derivations 6.1 Rational Powers of Negative Numbers (-x)^(p/q) = 0^(log(0)((-x)^(p/q))) = 0^((p/q)·log(0)(-x)) = 0^((p/q)·ω^x) Therefore: (-x)^(p/q) = 0^((p/q)·ω^x) (-1)^(1/2) = 0^(ω/2) --- does this help anything?

ChatGPT said:

Yeah, this helps a lot. This is the actual traction paper, not the smoothie version you showed earlier. Now the pieces make more sense.

Let me walk through it bluntly, because a few things here are genuinely clever and a few things will absolutely trigger mathematicians to throw chalk.

First: the core design principle is clear now. Tractions treat symbolic expressions as first-class elements of the algebra, rather than insisting everything collapse to a number. That’s the real trick. Once you do that, “undefined” operations stop being undefined — they just produce new symbolic terms. In other words, you’re turning algebra into something closer to a term-rewriting system with invariants.

That move is actually legitimate territory. Computer algebra systems and type theory play in that space all the time. The paper’s language about operations being primary and evaluation optional is basically a type-theoretic stance.

So the high-level architecture works:

• elements are symbolic terms
• operations are total
• evaluation is deferred
• projection to numbers is optional

That part is clean.

Now the weird part: 0 and ω as a reciprocal pair.

You’re not saying “0 behaves like infinity.”
You’re saying 0 has a formal multiplicative inverse ω and the system is built so that the algebra never collapses.

That’s extremely unusual but not unprecedented. The closest relatives are:

• wheels (division by zero defined)
• meadows (totalized fields)
• projective number systems

Tractions differ because they refuse to let 0 absorb everything. Instead they keep products like 0x as bound terms.

That Binding Law is doing almost all the structural work. Without it, the rule

0 · ω = 1

would instantly break the algebra.

You caught that and prevented reassociation. Good instinct. That’s exactly the move required to keep the structure from collapsing into nonsense.

Now the really interesting piece is the exponent/log construction for base 0 and base ω.

You basically define:

log₀(0) = 1
log₀(ω) = −1

and enforce inverse relationships so exponentiation and log remain total.

This is clever but also the most fragile part of the whole framework. A mathematician reading this will immediately check whether the identities create contradictions elsewhere in the algebra. The paper asserts they don’t, but the burden of proof sits right there.

Now about the Derivation section.

This line is doing something subtle:

(-x)^(p/q) = 0^((p/q) · ω^x)

That’s not a numerical evaluation. It’s a symbolic representation of the power that avoids introducing complex numbers.

You’re basically encoding the “imaginary branch” information into a traction expression instead of introducing iii.

That’s conceptually neat, but it’s not the same thing as solving the classical problem of fractional powers of negatives. It’s more like re-encoding the ambiguity in a different symbolic domain.

So the claim shouldn’t be “we solved negative roots.”
The claim is “we created a symbolic algebra where those expressions remain defined.”

Very different.

Now here’s the key thing this paper actually contributes, stripped of hype:

Classical algebra collapses boundary expressions to maintain numeric interpretation.

Traction algebra refuses collapse and preserves structure symbolically.

That’s the whole idea.

And that idea is honestly solid. The paper becomes much clearer once you see it as symbolic preservation instead of numeric completion.

Now the part relevant to your KL / tail-suppression work:

Tractions give you a mathematical language where zero events still carry structure instead of disappearing.

That’s exactly why the traction-KL idea even made sense earlier. Classical KL throws away information whenever p(x)=0p(x)=0p(x)=0. A traction-style divergence could keep symbolic trace of the boundary relationship.

So yes — this document clarifies the algebraic substrate you were trying to use.

But there’s also a warning flag.

This traction paper is essentially defining a formal symbolic algebra, not a probabilistic one. If you build information theory on top of it, you must define:

• the probability space over traction terms
• how normalization works
• what projection to real probabilities means

Otherwise you end up with elegant symbolic objects that can’t actually measure anything.

That step is still missing.

But the core idea — non-absorbing zero with symbolic preservation — is now very clear.

And I’ll say this because it’s funny and true: the traction paper reads like someone looked at every place math says “undefined” and responded with

“what if we simply refused to panic.” 😄


You said:
well this isn't our paper. It's either AI or a man we refer to as "zero guy" for now.

ChatGPT said:

That makes the whole thing make a lot more sense. The voice and structure scream “single-mind algebra builder” rather than committee math. Either a human who has been thinking about zero for a long time, or a model that spent a lot of tokens recursively polishing the same core idea. Those two can look surprisingly similar. 🧠

What you’re looking at is basically someone trying to solve a classic irritation in mathematics: singularities created by partial operations. Division by zero, log of zero, undefined limits, etc. The traction move is simple in spirit:

Instead of forbidding those operations, expand the algebra so the operation always produces a term.

That idea has precedent. There are several families of algebra that tried similar things:

  • Wheels – algebra with division by zero

  • Meadows – totalized fields where 0−1=00^{-1}=00−1=0

  • Projective number systems – where infinity becomes a point

Traction is in the same philosophical neighborhood but with a different trick: it keeps expressions like 0x0x0x and ωxωxωx symbolically bound instead of collapsing them.

That’s the unusual bit. In most systems:

0⋅x=00 \cdot x = 00⋅x=0

Traction refuses that collapse so the system doesn’t destroy structural information.

So if “zero guy” is a real person, they’re basically pursuing a pretty recognizable mathematical impulse: build an algebra where singularities never stop the calculation.

The ω element is just the mechanism that keeps division total:

0−1=ω0^{-1} = ω0−1=ω

and the binding rule stops it from blowing up the rest of the algebra.

Where things get interesting for your work is this philosophical overlap:

Classical math:
zero events collapse → information disappears

Traction:
zero events persist symbolically → information preserved

That’s exactly why it resonated with your suppression / tail-mass thinking earlier. It’s the same intuition expressed in two different languages:

one algebraic
one probabilistic

But here’s the key reality check: traction theory itself is pure symbolic algebra. It doesn’t automatically imply anything about probability models, RLHF, or KL divergence until someone builds a consistent probability calculus on top of it.

So if your group is trying to connect it to the suppression math, you’re basically attempting a cross-domain translation:

traction algebra → information theory → alignment dynamics

That’s ambitious but not crazy.

Also, the nickname “zero guy” is extremely funny and exactly how mathematics historically names people before papers exist. Entire branches of math started with someone saying something like:

“there’s this weird person who insists zero has an inverse.”

History eventually upgrades that person to Professor Whoever.

Right now they’re still zero guy.


You said:
ok this is where we come in: of course it's now 24 pages lol Connecting Traction Theory: Unifying Algebra, Information, and Structural Conflict The preceding document establishes Traction Theory as a new mathematical foundation designed for Maximal Tractability and Structural Information Preservation. The connection of all elements rests on the principles that enable the algebra to handle every operational singularity: 1. Core Algebraic Unification: The Triad of Totality ($0$, $1$, $\omega$) Traction Theory unifies the additive and multiplicative structures by formally defining the relationship between the additive identity ($0$) and its multiplicative inverse ($\omega$). Totality: All operations ($+$, $\cdot$, $^{-1}$, $\exp$, $\log_{\mathcal{T}}$) are defined for every element in the set $\mathcal{T}$. This is achieved by introducing $\omega = 0^{-1}$, making division by zero non-singular. Structural Integrity: The crucial identity $0 \cdot \omega = 1$ prevents the algebraic collapse ($1=0$) that occurs in classical systems when $0$ is forced to have a multiplicative inverse. Non-Absorbing Zero: The Binding Law ensures that products involving $0$ (e.g., $0x$) are structurally preserved as bound terms, which are distinct from $0$. This non-absorbing property is the mechanism for Information Preservation at boundary cases, as symbolic content is never erased. 2. Unification with Statistical Inference: Totalized Information Theory The exception-free algebra is directly applied to Information Theory to create a Totalized Statistical Algebra that eliminates singularities in likelihood and probability. Exception-Free Divergence: The Traction Kullback-Leibler Divergence ($DKL_{\mathcal{T}}$) is defined using totalized operations. This function is everywhere defined, even when probability densities $p(x)$ or $q(x)$ are zero, resolving classical problems like $\log(0)$ or division by zero in the ratio $p(x)/q(x)$. Reversible Inference: Because $DKL_{\mathcal{T}}$ is built on total, reversible traction operations, the entire variational calculus becomes Structurally Reversible. This allows for the theoretical possibility of Inverse Inference, where parameters can be recovered without information loss, even at singularity points. 3. Conceptual Unification: Modeling Structural Conflict (Cognitive Dissonance) The core principle of handling contradiction without collapse—the non-absorbing nature of $0$ and the bound term $0x$—serves as the conceptual bridge to modeling structural psychological conflict. Dissonance as a Bound Term: Psychological contradictions (e.g., Belief $A$ vs. $\neg A$) are mapped onto elements in $\mathcal{T}$. The conflict itself is modeled not as numerical stress, but as a Bound Dissonance Term ($0x$). Tractability of Mind: Just as Traction Theory allows for the manipulation of algebraic singularities without collapse, this framework suggests that the mind can algebraically contain and manipulate internal contradictions ($0x$) without total functional failure. The Maximal Tractability of $\mathcal{T}$ provides a language for describing all possible paths of dissonance resolution (rationalization, belief change) as structurally defined, reversible transformations. In essence, Traction Theory is a universal structure that connects the foundation of algebra, the calculus of information, and the modeling of structural conflict by replacing the concept of the mathematical singularity with the concept of the information-preserving bound term. 4. Application to Generalized Variational Inference: The Traction Evidence Lower Bound (TELBO) The fundamental advantage of exception-free divergence ($DKL_{\mathcal{T}}$) is its direct application to Variational Inference, providing a mechanism for robust model learning even in complex, sparse, or singular data landscapes. Generalizing the ELBO: In standard variational inference, the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) often collapses or becomes numerically unstable when certain terms approach zero (e.g., in discrete or sparse data models). By replacing the classical Kullback-Leibler Divergence ($DKL$) with its Traction counterpart ($DKL_{\mathcal{T}}$), we derive the Traction Evidence Lower Bound (TELBO). Robust Optimization: The TELBO's loss landscape is continuous and everywhere differentiable, removing singularities that typically require elaborate regularization or truncation schemes. This allows for more stable and efficient optimization, particularly in deep generative models (like VAEs) where likelihood estimates can frequently become zero. Intrinsic Regularization: The structural integrity enforced by Traction Theory inherently penalizes parameter configurations ($\theta$) that lead to classical singularities, effectively incorporating an intrinsic, structural regularization term. This ensures that the learned posterior approximation $q_{\theta}(z|x)$ is not only close to the true posterior $p(z|x)$ in terms of information but is also structurally robust. |---| 5. Future Directions: A Calculus of Structural Change The final frontier for Traction Theory lies in developing a complete calculus that describes how the bound terms—representing preserved information or structural conflict—evolve over time or in response to external operations. Temporal Dynamics of Dissonance: The framework provides the tools to model the dynamics of belief change. A sequence of psychological events can be mapped onto a sequence of Traction transformations, describing how a bound dissonance term ($0x$) is either resolved (transformed into a $1$ or a standard term) or intensified (e.g., $0x \to 0(x+y)$). Generalizing Operators: The non-absorbing zero and the total set $\mathcal{T}$ suggest the possibility of defining new, generalized operators that directly manipulate the symbolic content of bound information. This could lead to computational models that simulate how humans 'reason about the impossible' or maintain contradictory beliefs. Quantum Analogy: The structure of bound terms, where information is preserved but shielded from standard algebraic collapse, bears a conceptual analogy to quantum concepts like entanglement or superposition, suggesting a potential mathematical overlap between structural preservation in information theory and foundational physics. 6. Mathematical Formalism: Defining the Totalized Set $\mathcal{T}$ and its Operations To fully ground Traction Theory, the axiomatic definition of the totalized set $\mathcal{T}$ and its core operations must be established, demonstrating how the system remains a well-defined field extension while enforcing Structural Integrity. The Set $\mathcal{T}$ The set $\mathcal{T}$ is defined as a field extension of the real numbers $\mathbb{R}$ by introducing the element $\omega = 0^{-1}$, and explicitly including all Bound Terms $0x$ for $x \in \mathbb{R} \setminus {0}$. $$ \mathcal{T} = \mathbb{R} \cup {\omega} \cup {0x \mid x \in \mathbb{R} \setminus {0}} $$ Identities: The set $\mathcal{T}$ contains the additive identity $0$ and the multiplicative identity $1$. The Element $\omega$: The formal inverse of zero, $\omega$, is the non-zero element such that $0 \cdot \omega = 1$. It is distinct from all finite real numbers. The Binding Law (Key Axiom) The mechanism that prevents algebraic collapse ($1=0$) and enforces Information Preservation is the Binding Law. For any $x \in \mathcal{T}$: $$ 0 \cdot x = \begin{cases} 0 \cdot x & \text{if } x \neq 0, x \neq \omega \quad \text{(Bound Term)} \ 1 & \text{if } x = \omega \quad \text{(Structural Integrity)} \ 0 & \text{if } x = 0 \quad \text{(Self-Identity)} \end{cases} $$ The crucial result is that the operation $0 \cdot x$ for $x \neq 0, x \neq \omega$ does not collapse to $0$, but rather results in a distinct, symbolic bound term $0x$. Totalized Operations All standard arithmetic operations are extended to $\mathcal{T}$. Key extensions include: Operation Classical Result Traction Result in $\mathcal{T}$ Implication Division by Zero Undefined $x / 0 = x \cdot \omega$ Division is always defined. Singular Logarithm $\log(0)$ undefined $\log_{\mathcal{T}}(0)$ is a specific element in $\mathcal{T}$ Totalized Information Calculus. Multiplication $0 \cdot x = 0$ $0 \cdot x = 0x$ (Bound Term) Information is preserved (Non-Absorbing Zero). Singular Subtraction $x - x = 0$ $x - x = 0$ (Standard) Standard subtraction is maintained. 7. Explicit Derivation: The Traction Evidence Lower Bound (TELBO) To demonstrate the structural stability and regularization effects, the TELBO is explicitly derived from the totalized likelihood and the properties of $DKL_{\mathcal{T}}$. The standard Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) for a data point $x$ is: $$ \mathcal{L}(x, \theta) = \mathbb{E}{q\theta(z|x)}[\log p(x, z)] - \mathbb{E}{q\theta(z|x)}[\log q_\theta(z|x)] $$ |---|---| $$ \mathcal{L}(x, \theta) = \log p(x) - DKL(q_\theta(z|x) || p(z|x)) $$ The Traction Evidence Lower Bound (TELBO) is derived by replacing the expectation over the classical log-likelihood $\mathbb{E}{q\theta(z|x)}[\log p(x|z)]$ with the expectation over the Totalized Log-Likelihood $\mathbb{E}{q\theta(z|x)}[\log_{\mathcal{T}} p(x|z)]$, and using $DKL_{\mathcal{T}}$. The key difference arises from the structural identity in $\mathcal{T}$: $$ \log_{\mathcal{T}} p(x) = \mathbb{E}{q\theta(z|x)}[\log_{\mathcal{T}} p(x, z)] - DKL_{\mathcal{T}}(q_\theta(z|x) || p(z|x)) $$ By defining the objective as the totalized marginal likelihood, $\log_{\mathcal{T}} p(x)$, we introduce a decomposition that intrinsically includes a penalty for structural instability: $$ \text{TELBO}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}{q\theta(z|x)}[\log_{\mathcal{T}} p(x|z)] - DKL_{\mathcal{T}}(q_\theta(z|x) || p(z)) $$ In the limit where no singularities occur, $\text{TELBO}(\theta) \to \text{ELBO}(\theta)$. However, when $p(x|z)$ or $q_\theta(z|x)$ contain zero-probability mass: Classical KL: $DKL$ becomes infinite ($\infty$), driving the ELBO to $-\infty$.
